title: Psalms 82
subtitle: God Presides in the Great Assembly

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “How Long Will You Defend the Unjust?”

heb.paragraph: "God presides in the great assembly; he renders judgment among the ‘gods’ (ʾĕlōhîm)" (v.1). The most debated psalm. Who are the "gods"? Options: human judges, angelic beings, or pagan deities. Jesus quotes v.6 in John 10:34. God commands them: defend the weak, maintain the rights of the poor (v.3). "They know nothing, they understand nothing" (v.5).

notes.note: "God presides in the great assembly." The divine council: God among the ʾĕlōhîm. Whether human judges or heavenly beings, God holds them accountable.
"How long will you defend the unjust? Defend the weak and the fatherless; uphold the cause of the poor." God’s demand: justice for the vulnerable. The judges have failed.

sections.header: Verses 5–8 — “You Are Gods, Yet You Will Die Like Mere Mortals”

heb.paragraph: "I said, ‘You are “gods”; you are all sons of the Most High.’ But you will die (təmūṭūn) like mere mortals" (vv.6–7). Jesus quotes this in John 10:34–36 to argue: if Scripture calls human judges "gods," how much more the one the Father sanctified? The psalm closes: "Rise up, O God, judge the earth, for all the nations are your inheritance" (v.8). God alone is the true judge.

notes.note: "You are gods... but you will die like mere mortals." The title "gods" is revoked by mortality. Whatever status they held, death equalises. Jesus uses this argument (John 10:34–36).
"Rise up, O God, judge the earth, for all the nations are your inheritance." Since the delegated judges failed, God himself must judge. The psalm ends with a summons for divine intervention.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " The divine council: God among the ʾĕlōhîm…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVGod presides in the great assembly; he renders judgment among the “gods”:ESVGod has taken his place in the divine council; in the midst of the gods he holds judgment:NIVDefend the weak and the fatherless; uphold the cause of the poor and the oppressed.ESVGive justice to the weak and the fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute.

rec.text: ""God presides in the great assembly." The divine council: God among the ʾĕlōhîm. Whether human judges or heavenly beings, God holds them accountable."

rows.label: The Failed Judges — 82:1–4
rows.text: "God presides among the ‘gods.’" Defend the weak! "They know nothing."

hebtext.note: "God presides in the great assembly; he renders judgment among the ‘gods’ (ʾĕlōhîm)" (v.1). The most debated psalm. Who are the "gods"? Options: human judges, angelic beings, or pagan deities. Jesus quotes v.6 in John 10:34. God commands them: defend the weak, maintain the rights of the poor (v.3). "They know nothing, they understand nothing" (v.5).

"I said, ‘You are “gods”; you are all sons of the Most High.’ But you will die (təmūṭūn) like mere mortals" (vv.6–7). Jesus quotes this in John 10:34–36 to argue: if Scripture calls human judges "gods," how much more the one the Father sanctified? The psalm closes: "Rise up, O God, judge the earth, for all the nations are your inheritance" (v.8). God alone is the true judge.

themes.note: Justice peaks at 10: failed judges indicted. Judgment at 9: mortality revokes the title "gods." Prophecy: Jesus quotes v.6 (John 10:34).
